description: Pfeiffer Big Sur, Julia Pfeiffer Burns, and Andrew Molera state parks charge day-use vehicle fees. Garrapata is free. Point Lobos charges a separate entry fee.
cost: $8–$12 per vehicle (varies by park) · Point Lobos $10/vehicle

seasonalWindow.notes: Fee applies at all staffed entrance stations. Garrapata State Park has no entrance fee.

tips: The California State Parks annual pass ($125) covers day-use at all parks in the corridor.
The America the Beautiful pass ($80/year) covers some federal sites but NOT California State Parks.
Point Lobos limits to 420 vehicles — arrive before 10am on weekends or park on Highway 1 and walk in.
Pfeiffer Beach (accessed via Sycamore Canyon Rd) charges $12/vehicle, cash only.

description: Pfeiffer Big Sur State Park campground (218 sites) is the most popular in California. Reservations open 6 months in advance to the day and book within minutes. Andrew Molera has primitive hike-in sites.

seasonalWindow.notes: Summer and spring weekends are most competitive. Set a calendar alert for your 6-month booking window.

tips: Reservations open at 8am PT, 6 months in advance — be online at exactly 8am.
Weekday reservations are significantly easier to secure.
Andrew Molera's hike-in campsites are first-come, first-served and rarely full on weekdays.
Winter camping is available but some sites close seasonally — check ahead.

activity: National Forest Campground Reservations (Kirk Creek, Plaskett Creek)
description: Kirk Creek (33 clifftop sites above the Pacific) and Plaskett Creek campgrounds are managed by the USDA Forest Service. Reservations via recreation.gov, 6 months in advance.

seasonalWindow.notes: Kirk Creek is the most sought-after national forest campground on the coast — every site has ocean views.

tips: Book 6 months out on recreation.gov — popular weekends sell out immediately.
Kirk Creek has no hookups, no showers — pack accordingly.
The clifftop setting makes Kirk Creek among the best stargazing campgrounds in California.
Plaskett Creek is slightly less competitive and has more tree cover.

description: Overnight backcountry camping in the Ventana Wilderness requires a free wilderness permit from the USFS King City Ranger District. Day hiking does not require a permit.
where: USFS King City Ranger District office or self-register at trailheads

seasonalWindow.notes: Fire restrictions may close areas during summer/fall. Check current conditions with the ranger district.

tips: Self-registration available at some trailheads — fill out the form at the register box.
Check fire season closures before planning backcountry trips (typically July–October).
Trail conditions vary — consult ventanawild.org for current reports.
Pack a bear canister — black bears are present in the backcountry.

description: Diving at Point Lobos State Natural Reserve (Whalers and Bluefish Coves) requires advance reservations. Limited to a small number of dive teams per day.

seasonalWindow.notes: Reservations are competitive — call well in advance, especially for weekends.

tips: Only Whalers Cove and Bluefish Cove are open to diving.
Water temperature is 50°F year-round — drysuit strongly recommended.
The kelp forests here are among the richest dive environments in North America.
Arrive early on your dive day — parking at Point Lobos fills quickly.
